Source:	https://github.com/wandb/examples/tree/master/colabs/wandb-model-registry/Model_Registry_E2E.ipynb

Log Data and Model Checkpoints as Artifacts


W&B Artifacts allows you to track and version arbitrary serialized data (e.g. datasets, model checkpoints, evaluation results). When you create an artifact, you give it a name and a type, and that artifact is forever linked to the experimental system of record. If the underlying data changes, and you log that data asset again, W&B will automatically create new versions through checksummming its contents. W&B Artifacts can be thought of as a lightweight abstraction layer on top of shared unstructured file systems.

\* a name  

\* a type  

\* metadata  

\* description  

\* files, directory of files, or references

run = wandb.init(project = "my-project")
artifact = wandb.Artifact(name = "my_artifact", type = "data")
artifact.add_file("/path/to/my/file.txt")
run.log_artifact(artifact)

question: Is it possible to embed a Weights & Biases plot inside a webpage without using a report?
answer:
Yes, it is possible to embed a Weights & Biases (W&B) plot inside a webpage without using a report. W&B provides a feature to create sharable links for individual charts and plots that you can embed in webpages, similar to how you might embed a YouTube video.

To embed a W&B plot in a webpage, you can follow these general steps:

1. Go to your W&B dashboard and navigate to the project and run that contains the plot you want to embed.
2. Click on the plot to open it in a larger view.
3. Look for a sharing option or menu within the plot's interface. There should be an option to create a shareable link or to get the embed code.
4. Copy the embed code provided by W&B. It will typically be an iframe HTML element that you can insert into your webpage's HTML code.
5. Paste the iframe code into the HTML of your webpage where you want the plot to appear.

Please note that the exact steps may vary depending on the version of the W&B interface and the features available at the time. If you're unable to find the embed option, you may need to consult the latest W&B documentation or reach out to their support for assistance.

Keep in mind that the visibility of the embedded plot will depend on the privacy settings of your W&B project. If the project is private, you may need to adjust the settings to allow others to view the plot without requiring them to log in to W&B.

What is W&B?


Weights & Biases (W&B) is the AI developer platform, with tools for training models, fine-tuning models, and leveraging foundation models. 


Set up W&B in 5 minutes, then quickly iterate on your machine learning pipeline with the confidence that your models and data are tracked and versioned in a reliable system of record.

Source:	https://docs.wandb.ai/guides/integrations/hydra

Optimize Hyperparameters


W&B Sweeps is a highly scalable hyperparameter search platform, which provides interesting insights and visualization about W&B experiments with minimal requirements code real-estate. Sweeps integrates seamlessly with Hydra projects with no-coding requirements. The only thing needed is a configuration file describing the various parameters to sweep over as normal.


A simple example `sweep.yaml` file would be:



```
program: main.py
method: bayes
metric:
  goal: maximize
  name: test/accuracy
parameters:
  dataset:
    values: [mnist, cifar10]

command:
  - ${env}
  - python
  - ${program}
  - ${args_no_hyphens}

```

Invoke the sweep with:


`wandb sweep sweep.yaml`\  

`\`Once you call this, W&B automatically creates a sweep inside your project and returns a `wandb agent` command for you to run on each machine you want to run your sweep.

question: If `wandb.markdown` is an actual method like you say, then why do I get the error "`AttributeError: module 'wandb' has no attribute 'markdown'`"?
answer:
It appears there has been a misunderstanding. `wandb.markdown` is not a method in the Weights & Biases (wandb) library. If you are receiving the error "`AttributeError: module 'wandb' has no attribute 'markdown'`", it means that you are trying to access an attribute called `markdown` from the `wandb` module, which does not exist. Weights & Biases is a tool for tracking and visualizing machine learning experiments, and while it does support logging various types of data, including text and markdown for reports, it does not provide a method called `markdown` directly on the `wandb` module. If you are trying to log markdown text to Weights & Biases, you can use the `wandb.log` method to log text data, which can include markdown formatting. Here's an example of how you might log markdown-formatted text: ```python import wandb # Initialize a new run wandb.init(project="my_project") # Log some markdown-formatted text wandb.log({"example_markdown": wandb.Html("

Header

This is a markdown example

")}) # Finish the run wandb.finish() ``` In this example, `wandb.Html` is used to log HTML, which can be used to render markdown in the Weights & Biases UI. If you are looking to log markdown or rich text, make sure to use the appropriate logging method provided by the `wandb` library.

question: Is there any way to block the "only fetch these keys, and only fetch rows that have all of keys defined" -behaviour of scan_history such that all rows are returned, even if not all keys are defined in a certain row?
answer:
The `scan_history` method in the Weights & Biases (W&B) API allows you to fetch historical data from a run. By default, if you specify keys in the `keys` argument, `scan_history` will only return rows where all of the specified keys are defined. If you want to fetch all rows, regardless of whether all keys are defined in each row, you can omit the `keys` argument or set it to `None`.

Here's how you can use `scan_history` to fetch all rows:

```python
run = api.run("entity/project/run_id")
history = run.scan_history()  # No keys argument provided
all_rows = [row for row in history]
```

In this case, `scan_history` will return all rows, and it will be up to you to handle any missing keys in the rows as you iterate through the history records.

If you want to fetch rows that contain any of the specified keys (not necessarily all of them), you would need to fetch all rows and then filter them manually in your code. Here's an example of how you might do that:

```python
run = api.run("entity/project/run_id")
history = run.scan_history()  # No keys argument provided
desired_keys = ["Loss", "Accuracy", "Validation Loss"]  # Example keys
filtered_rows = []

for row in history:
    # Check if any of the desired keys are in the row
    if any(key in row for key in desired_keys):
        filtered_rows.append(row)

# Now filtered_rows contains all rows where at least one of the desired keys is present
```

This way, you can customize the behavior to return rows that contain any subset of the keys you are interested in.

question: How do I authenticate with W&B when doing training  on a cloud service? Does every user using the service to train have to authenticate personally even when we have a team account? Only the training code runs on the cloud server, the user is not logged in to the server machines. The user only uses a client application to initiate the training. How will the server authenticate?
answer:
When training on a cloud service using W&B, you typically authenticate by setting an environment variable with your API key. This allows the training code running on the cloud server to authenticate with W&B without requiring each user to log in manually. If you have a team account, you can use service accounts to manage access to the W&B API without sharing personal API keys.

Here's how you can handle authentication in a team setting with a cloud service:

1. **Service Accounts**: Create a service account in W&B for your team and use the API key associated with that account. This way, you don't have to use personal API keys for each user. Service accounts are meant for scenarios like this, where automated processes or machines need to authenticate with W&B.

2. **Environment Variables**: Set the `WANDB_API_KEY` environment variable on the cloud server with the service account's API key. This can be done in the cloud service's job specification or configuration. For example, if you're using AWS, you can set the environment variable in the AWS batch job spec.

3. **Security**: Ensure that the API key is stored securely and is not exposed in logs or checked into version control. Use the cloud service's recommended method for setting secure environment variables.

4. **Running the Training**: When the user initiates training through the client application, the training code on the server will automatically use the `WANDB_API_KEY` environment variable to authenticate with W&B.

Here's an example of how you might set the environment variable in your cloud service:

```bash
export WANDB_API_KEY=your_service_account_api_key
```

And in your training code, you would have something like this:

```python
import wandb

# This will use the WANDB_API_KEY from the environment variable
wandb.login()
```

By using a service account and setting the `WANDB_API_KEY` environment variable on the cloud server, you can ensure that all training runs are authenticated and logged to your W&B team account without requiring each user to authenticate individually. This setup is particularly useful for automated training pipelines and batch processing jobs.

question: I would like to finish a run and remove all hooks that may be attached to my torch modules and remove any other affects that wandb might still has on my code after calling wand.finish(). How do i do that?
answer:
To finish a run and clean up any effects that W&B might still have on your code, including removing hooks from PyTorch modules, you can follow these steps:

1. **Finish the Run**: First, ensure you call `wandb.finish()` to end the run. This will mark the run as finished and finish uploading all data.

    ```python
    import wandb
    # ... your training loop and logging code ...
    wandb.finish()
    ```

2. **Remove PyTorch Hooks**: If you have hooks attached to your PyTorch modules, you'll need to remove them manually. PyTorch doesn't provide a built-in method to remove all hooks at once, so you'll need to keep track of the hook handles when you register them and remove them individually.

    Here's an example of how you might add and then remove a hook:

    ```python
    import torch

    # Example forward hook function
    def forward_hook(module, input, output):
        print("Forward hook called.")

    # Registering the hook on a module and keeping the handle
    module = torch.nn.Linear(10, 5)
    hook_handle = module.register_forward_hook(forward_hook)

    # ... your training loop ...

    # Removing the hook using the handle
    hook_handle.remove()
    ```

    If you have multiple hooks or modules, you might want to create a list or dictionary to store all the handles and iterate over it to remove the hooks.

3. **Reset W&B State**: After calling `wandb.finish()`, the W&B run should be cleaned up automatically. However, if you want to ensure that the W&B state is reset, you can manually set `wandb.run` to `None`.

    ```python
    wandb.run = None
    ```

4. **Disable W&B Logging**: If you want to disable W&B logging after finishing the run, you can set the logging level to ERROR as shown in the context information:

    ```python
    import logging
    logger = logging.getLogger("wandb")
    logger.setLevel(logging.ERROR)
    ```

5. **Remove W&B Environment Variables**: If you've set any W&B environment variables in your script or Jupyter notebook, you can unset them or set them to empty strings to remove their effects.

    ```python
    import os
    os.environ.pop("WANDB_SILENT", None)  # Remove the WANDB_SILENT variable if it exists
    ```

By following these steps, you should be able to finish your W&B run, remove any hooks from PyTorch modules, and clean up any residual effects of W&B in your code. Remember that if you're using W&B in a Jupyter notebook or an environment where the Python process is not exiting, you may need to take additional steps to reset the state as the environment may persist state across cell executions.

question: how do I fix an error with wandb Table construction from pandas dataframe: TypeError: Data row contained incompatible types
answer:
When constructing a `wandb.Table` from a pandas DataFrame, a `TypeError` indicating that a data row contained incompatible types usually means that there is a mismatch between the data types that `wandb.Table` expects and the actual data types present in the DataFrame.

Here are some steps you can take to troubleshoot and fix this error:

1. **Check Data Types in DataFrame**: Ensure that the data types in your DataFrame are compatible with the types that `wandb.Table` supports. For example, `wandb.Table` supports numeric, text, boolean, image, video, and audio types. If your DataFrame contains other types (like complex objects, lists, or dictionaries), you may need to convert them to a string or another compatible format before logging.

2. **Convert Unsupported Types**: If you have columns with unsupported types, convert them to a string or another supported type. For example, if you have a column with datetime objects, you can convert it to a string representation of the date:

   ```python
   df['date_column'] = df['date_column'].astype(str)
   ```

3. **Handle Missing Values**: If your DataFrame contains missing values (`NaN`), make sure to handle them appropriately. You can fill them with a default value or remove the rows with missing data.

4. **Explicitly Define Columns**: When creating the `wandb.Table`, you can explicitly define the columns and their types to ensure compatibility. This can be done by passing the `columns` argument to the `wandb.Table` constructor.

5. **Log a Subset of Columns**: If you suspect that only certain columns are causing the issue, try logging a subset of the DataFrame that excludes those columns to identify the problematic ones.

6. **Check for Mixed Types in Columns**: Sometimes a single DataFrame column may contain mixed types, which can cause issues. Ensure that each column contains a single data type.

7. **Use the Latest Version of W&B**: Ensure that you are using the latest version of the `wandb` library, as updates may include fixes for issues related to data logging.

8. **Log Data Row by Row**: If you continue to have issues, you can try adding data to the `wandb.Table` row by row, which can help you identify the exact row that is causing the problem.

Here's an example of how you might log a DataFrame to `wandb.Table` while handling potential type issues:

```python
import wandb
import pandas as pd

# Assuming df is your pandas DataFrame
# Convert all columns to string to avoid type issues
df = df.astype(str)

# Handle missing values by replacing them with a placeholder
df.fillna("N/A", inplace=True)

# Create a W&B Table from the DataFrame
table = wandb.Table(dataframe=df)

# Log the table
run = wandb.init(project="your_project")
run.log({"my_table": table})
run.finish()
```

If you've tried these steps and are still encountering issues, you may want to inspect individual rows and columns to identify the exact source of the type incompatibility. Once identified, you can apply a targeted fix to ensure that all data conforms to the types supported by `wandb.Table`.

iris_table_artifact.add_file("iris.csv")

question: what is the benefit of wandb compared to MLFlow? Explain it to me like I am five years old.
answer:
Imagine you have a big box of crayons (these are like your machine learning models) and you love to draw lots of pictures (these are like your machine learning experiments). Now, you want to make sure you can find your favorite crayons again and remember which ones you used for your best drawings.

W&B (Weights & Biases) is like a magical coloring book that not only lets you draw but also helps you keep track of all your crayons and drawings. It's like having a smart helper who takes photos of your drawings, notes down which crayons you used, and even tells you if some crayons seem to work better than others. This way, you can always look back and see what you did to make a really cool picture.

MLFlow is another magical coloring book that can do many similar things. It also helps you keep track of your crayons and drawings. But W&B has some extra sparkles that make it even more fun and easy to use. For example, W&B can make your drawings move (like animations to show how your picture changes over time), and it's really good at sharing your drawings with friends so they can see how you made them.

Both magical coloring books are great, but W&B might have a few more stickers and glitter pens that make your drawing time even more special and help you make even more amazing art!

question:
I started the Sweep on one machine, that is logged into a certain wandb account. I saved the sweep_id, and passed it on to another wandb account which also tried to run the same Sweep. I do this because I want multiple machines to run the same Sweep to aggregate their results. However, when trying to run the same Sweep as the first machine on the second with the set sweep_id, I get the error: 

'wandb.errors.UsageError: could not find project project-name/uncategorized during createAgent'
answer:
The error message you're encountering suggests that the second machine, which is logged into a different Weights & Biases (W&B) account, is unable to find the project associated with the `sweep_id` you provided. This can happen if the project is private or if the second account does not have the necessary permissions to access the project.

Here are some steps you can take to resolve this issue:

1. **Check Project Visibility**: Ensure that the project associated with the `sweep_id` is set to public or that the second W&B account has been invited to the project with the appropriate permissions.

2. **Verify Sweep ID**: Double-check the `sweep_id` you are using to ensure it is correct and corresponds to the sweep you initialized on the first machine. The `sweep_id` should be in the format `entity/project/sweep_ID`.

3. **Add Second Account to Team**: If the project is under a team account, you may need to add the second W&B account to the team with the appropriate permissions to access the sweep.

4. **Use the Same W&B Account**: If possible, log into the same W&B account on the second machine that you used to create the sweep on the first machine. This will ensure that the second machine has access to the sweep without any additional configuration.

5. **Check Network Configuration**: Ensure that the second machine has proper network access to the W&B servers and that there are no firewalls or network policies preventing access.

6. **Update W&B Library**: Make sure you are using the latest version of the W&B library on both machines, as updates may contain important fixes or changes to the API.

7. **Contact W&B Support**: If you continue to face issues, consider reaching out to W&B support for assistance. They may be able to provide more specific guidance based on your account and project settings.

To add a user to a project or team, the owner or an admin of the project/team can invite the user from the W&B web interface. Once the second account has been added with the correct permissions, you should be able to run the sweep on multiple machines and aggregate the results as intended.

Start the Sweep


Use the `wandb.agent` API call to start a sweep.



```
wandb.agent(sweep_id, function=main, count=10)

Source:	https://docs.wandb.ai/guides/sweeps/walkthrough

Walkthrough


  

Sweeps Walkthrough  




This page shows how to define, initialize, and run a sweep. There are four main steps:


1. Set up your training code
2. Define the search space with a sweep configuration
3. Initialize the sweep
4. Start the sweep agent


Copy and paste the following code into a Jupyter Notebook or Python script:



```
# Import the W&B Python Library and log into W&B
import wandb

wandb.login()


# 1: Define objective/training function
def objective(config):
    score = config.x**3 + config.y
    return score


def main():
    wandb.init(project="my-first-sweep")
    score = objective(wandb.config)
    wandb.log({"score": score})


# 2: Define the search space
sweep_configuration = {
    "method": "random",
    "metric": {"goal": "minimize", "name": "score"},
    "parameters": {
        "x": {"max": 0.1, "min": 0.01},
        "y": {"values": [1, 3, 7]},
    },
}

# 3: Start the sweep
sweep_id = wandb.sweep(sweep=sweep_configuration, project="my-first-sweep")

wandb.agent(sweep_id, function=main, count=10)

```

The following sections break down and explains each step in the code sample.

---

Source:	https://docs.wandb.ai/guides/sweeps/start-sweep-agents

Start sweep agents


  

Start or stop a W&B Sweep  




Start a W&B Sweep on one or more agents on one or more machines. W&B Sweep agents query the W&B server you launched when you initialized a W&B Sweep (`wandb sweep)` for hyperparameters and use them to run model training.


To start a W&B Sweep agent, provide the W&B Sweep ID that was returned when you initialized a W&B Sweep. The W&B Sweep ID has the form:



```
entity/project/sweep_ID

```

Where:


* entity: Your W&B username or team name.
* project: The name of the project where you want the output of the W&B Run to be stored. If the project is not specified, the run is put in an “Uncategorized” project.
* sweep\_ID: The pseudo random, unique ID generated by W&B.


Provide the name of the function the W&B Sweep will execute if you start a W&B Sweep agent within a Jupyter Notebook or Python script.


The proceeding code snippets demonstrate how to start an agent with W&B. We assume you already have a configuration file and you have already initialized a W&B Sweep. For more information about how to define a configuration file, see Define sweep configuration.



Use the `wandb agent` command to start a sweep. Provide the sweep ID that was returned when you initialized the sweep. Copy and paste the code snippet below and replace `sweep_id` with your sweep ID:



```
wandb agent sweep_id

```


Use the W&B Python SDK library to start a sweep. Provide the sweep ID that was returned when you initialized the sweep. In addition, provide the name of the function the sweep will execute.



```
wandb.agent(sweep_id=sweep_id, function=function_name)

```

---

Source:	https://github.com/wandb/examples/tree/master/colabs/boosting/Using_W&B_Sweeps_with_XGBoost.ipynb

2. Initialize the Sweep


Calling `wandb.sweep` starts a Sweep Controller –  

a centralized process that provides settings of the `parameters` to any who query it  

and expects them to return performance on `metrics` via `wandb` logging.



```
sweep_id = wandb.sweep(sweep_config, project="XGBoost-sweeps")

```

---

Source:	https://github.com/wandb/examples/tree/master/colabs/pytorch-lightning/Fine_tuning_a_Transformer_with_Pytorch_Lightning.ipynb

Running a Hyperparameter Sweep 🧹


W&B sweeps allow you to optimize your model hyperparameters with minimal effort. In general, the workflow of sweeps is:  

1. Construct a dictionary or YAML file that defines the hyperparameter space   

2. Call `wandb.sweep(<sweep-dict>)` from the python library or `wandb sweep <yaml-file>` from the command line to initialize the sweep in W&B  

3. Run `wandb.agent(<sweep-id>)` (python lib) or `wandb agent <sweep-id>` (cli) to start a sweep agent to continuously:  

 - pull hyperparameter combinations from W&B  

 - run training with the given hyperparameters   

 - log training metrics back to W&B



We implement the sweeps workflow laid out above by:  

1. Creating a `sweep_config` dictionary describing our hyperparameter space and objective  

 - The hyperparameters we will sweep over are `learning_rate`, `batch_size`, and `epochs`  

 - Our objective in this sweep is to maximize the `validation/epoch_acc` metric logged to W&B  

 - We will use the `random` strategy, which means we will sample uniformly from the parameter space indefinitely  

2. Calling `wandb.sweep(sweep_config)` to create the sweep in our W&B project  

 - `wandb.sweep` will return a unique id for the sweep, saved as `sweep_id`  

3. Calling `wandb.agent(sweep_id, function=train)` to create an agent that will execute training with different hyperparameter combinations  

 - The agent will repeatedly query W&B for hyperparameter combinations  

 - When `wandb.init` is called within an agent, the `config` dictionary of the returned `run` will be populated with the next hyperparameter combination in the sweep



```
sweep_config = {
    'method': 'random',  # Randomly sample the hyperparameter space (alternatives: grid, bayes)
    'metric': {  # This is the metric we are interested in maximizing
      'name': 'validation/epoch_acc',
      'goal': 'maximize'   
    },
    # Paramters and parameter values we are sweeping across
    'parameters': {
        'learning_rate': {
            'values': [5e-5, 3e-5, 2e-5]
        },
        'batch_size': {
            'values': [16, 32]
        },
        'epochs':{
            'values': [1, 2]
        }
    }
}

```


```
# Create the sweep
sweep_id = wandb.sweep(sweep_config, project=project, entity=entity)

```


```
# Run an agent 🕵️ to try out 5 hyperparameter combinations
wandb.agent(sweep_id, function=train, count=5)

```

---
notes: The answer provides a comprehensive explanation of potential steps to resolve the issue described in the question, closely aligning with the operations and considerations outlined in the documentation regarding the initialization and running of sweeps in Weights & Biases. The explanation regarding project visibility and permissions, sweep ID verification, team membership, and contacting support are all relevant to addressing the problem of a UsageError when attempting to join a sweep from a different account.

question: I want to download the plots I see in the web UI. Is there a way to reference a plot in python and get a pdf or eps directly instead?
answer:
As of my knowledge cutoff in early 2023, the WandB (Weights & Biases) documentation does not provide a direct method to programmatically download plots from the web UI as PDF or EPS files using Python. However, you can programmatically create reports and visualizations using the WandB API, and then save those visualizations locally.

Here's a general approach to create and save a plot using the WandB API:

1. Use the WandB API to query the data you want to plot.
2. Create the plot using a plotting library like `matplotlib`, `seaborn`, or `plotly`.
3. Save the plot to a file in your desired format (PDF, EPS, PNG, etc.).

Here's an example using `matplotlib` to create a plot and save it as a PDF:

```python
import wandb
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Initialize a WandB run
wandb.init(project='your_project_name')

# Query the data you want to plot (this is just an example)
data = wandb.Api().run("your_entity/your_project_name/run_id").history(keys=["accuracy"])

# Create a plot
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
plt.plot(data['accuracy'])
plt.title('Model Accuracy')
plt.xlabel('Epochs')
plt.ylabel('Accuracy')

# Save the plot as a PDF
plt.savefig('model_accuracy.pdf', format='pdf')

# Optionally, log the plot to WandB
wandb.log({"accuracy_plot": wandb.Image('model_accuracy.pdf')})

# Finish the run
wandb.finish()
```

If you specifically want to reference and download a plot that already exists in the web UI, you would typically need to use the browser's functionality to save the image or use a screenshot tool. Some plotting libraries may also provide a way to export visualizations directly from the web UI, but this would be specific to the library and not a feature of WandB itself.

For more advanced use cases, you might consider using WandB's API to extract the data behind the plots and then recreate and save them using your preferred plotting library in Python.

question: I'm getting `Artifact <artifact_name> already exists with type <old_type>; cannot create another with type <new_type>` although I deleted the <artifact_name> with the <old_type> in the UI and I can't see it any more. what else do I need to do to be able to create an artifact with the same name but the other type?
answer:
It seems like you're encountering an issue where the artifact with the old type still exists in some form in the system, even though you've deleted it from the UI. This could be due to caching or a delay in the propagation of the deletion through the system.

Here are a few steps you can take to troubleshoot and resolve this issue:

1. **Wait and Retry**: Sometimes, it takes a little time for the deletion to propagate through the system. Wait for a few minutes and then try creating the artifact with the new type again.

2. **Check for Hidden Versions**: Ensure that all versions of the artifact have been deleted. Sometimes, an artifact may not be completely removed if there are still versions of it that haven't been deleted. Go through the deletion process again and make sure that all versions are deleted.

3. **Use a Different Name**: If waiting doesn't help and you're sure that all versions are deleted, consider using a slightly different name for your artifact to avoid naming conflicts. This is a workaround that can unblock you if you need to proceed immediately.

4. **Contact Support**: If none of the above steps work, there might be an issue with the W&B backend that requires intervention from their support team. Reach out to W&B support with details of the issue, including the artifact name, the old type, the new type, and the steps you've taken to delete the artifact.

5. **Check the API**: If you're comfortable with using the W&B API, you can try to query the artifact using the API to see if it still exists in the system. If it does, you might be able to force delete it using the API.

Remember that artifact names are unique within a project, and you cannot create an artifact with the same name but a different type. This uniqueness constraint is in place to maintain the integrity of the artifact lineage and prevent confusion in the artifact graph.

Source:	https://docs.wandb.ai/guides/artifacts/delete-artifacts

Delete an artifact version


To delete an artifact version:


1. Select the name of the artifact. This will expand the artifact view and list all the artifact versions associated with that artifact.
2. From the list of artifacts, select the artifact version you want to delete.
3. On the right hand side of the workspace, select the kebab dropdown.
4. Choose Delete.


An artifact version can also be deleted programatically via the delete() method. See the examples below.

---

Source:	https://docs.wandb.ai/guides/artifacts/create-a-new-artifact-version

Create new artifact versions from scratch


There are two ways to create a new artifact version: from a single run and from distributed runs. They are defined as follows:


* **Single run**: A single run provides all the data for a new version. This is the most common case and is best suited when the run fully recreates the needed data. For example: outputting saved models or model predictions in a table for analysis.
* **Distributed runs**: A set of runs collectively provides all the data for a new version. This is best suited for distributed jobs which have multiple runs generating data, often in parallel. For example: evaluating a model in a distributed manner, and outputting the predictions.


W&B will create a new artifact and assign it a `v0` alias if you pass a name to the `wandb.Artifact` API that does not exist in your project. W&B checksums the contents when you log again to the same artifact. If the artifact changed, W&B saves a new version `v1`. 


W&B will retrieve an existing artifact if you pass a name and artifact type to the `wandb.Artifact` API that matches an existing artifact in your project. The retrieved artifact will have a version greater than 1.

* **Type**: Provide a type. The type should be simple, descriptive and correspond to a single step of your machine learning pipeline. Common artifact types include `'dataset'` or `'model'`.


:::tip  

The “name” and “type” you provide is used to create a directed acyclic graph. This means you can view the lineage of an artifact on the W&B App. 


See the Explore and traverse artifact graphs for more information.

Artifacts can not have the same name, even if you specify a different type for the types parameter. In other words, you can not create an artifact named ‘cats’ of type ‘dataset’ and another artifact with the same name of type ‘model’.

question: I really like the random names generated by wandb for every new run, however I mostly deal with grouped runs (for kfold training). Although we can name the groups using a custom name, is it possible to use the same engine that wandb uses for naming the runs, to name the groups instead.
answer:
As of my knowledge cutoff in early 2023, Weights & Biases (wandb) does not provide a built-in feature to automatically generate random names for groups similar to the random names it generates for individual runs. However, you can create a custom function in your script to generate random names for your groups using a similar approach to how wandb generates random names for runs.

Here's a Python function that mimics the style of wandb's random run names, which typically combine two words with a hyphen:

```python
import random

def generate_random_group_name():
    # List of adjectives and nouns for random name generation
    adjectives = ["eager", "fast", "gentle", "hidden", "icy", "jolly", "kind", "long", "merry", "noisy", "old", "proud", "quiet", "rare", "swift", "tall"]
    nouns = ["waterfall", "river", "breeze", "moon", "rain", "wind", "sea", "morning", "snow", "lake", "sunset", "pine", "shadow", "leaf", "dawn", "glitter"]

    # Select one adjective and one noun at random
    adj = random.choice(adjectives)
    noun = random.choice(nouns)

    # Combine them to create a group name
    group_name = f"{adj}-{noun}"
    return group_name

# Example usage
group_name = generate_random_group_name()
print(group_name)  # Outputs a random group name like "swift-river"
```

You can then use this function to set the group name when initializing your wandb runs:

```python
import wandb

# Generate a random group name
group_name = generate_random_group_name()

# Initialize wandb with the random group name
wandb.init(group=group_name)
```

This way, each time you start a new group of runs, you can call `generate_random_group_name()` to get a unique and random group name that has a similar style to wandb's run names. If you want to ensure that the names are unique across multiple runs of your script, you might want to store the used names and check against them or include a timestamp or unique identifier in the group name.

question: i am using  init_kwargs with accelerator for main process in my training network script. how can i track notes in the accelerate launch args?
answer:
To track notes in your Weights & Biases (W&B) run when using Hugging Face Accelerate, you can include the `notes` parameter within the `init_kwargs` argument when calling `accelerator.init_trackers()`. The `init_kwargs` is a dictionary that allows you to pass additional arguments to `wandb.init()`, which is the function that initializes a new W&B run.

Here's an example of how you can include notes in your W&B run:

```python
from accelerate import Accelerator

# Initialize the Accelerator
accelerator = Accelerator(log_with="wandb")

# Define your notes
my_notes = "This is an example note for my training run."

# Initialize W&B trackers with additional arguments
accelerator.init_trackers(
    project_name="my_project",
    config={"dropout": 0.1, "learning_rate": 1e-2},
    init_kwargs={"wandb": {"entity": "my-wandb-team", "notes": my_notes}}
)

# Rest of your training script...
```

In this example, `my_notes` is a string that contains the notes you want to track. These notes will be associated with your W&B run and will be visible on the W&B dashboard.

Remember that the `init_kwargs` argument is a nested dictionary where the outer key is `"wandb"` and the inner keys are the parameters you want to pass to `wandb.init()`. The `notes` parameter is one of the many parameters you can set to customize your W&B run.

Make sure to include this code before starting your training loop and after initializing the Accelerator object. This way, when the W&B run is created, it will include the notes you've specified.

Training and Image Classifier



```
!pip install accelerate wandb torcheval timm fastprogress

```


```
import os
from types import SimpleNamespace

import wandb

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader
from torch.optim import AdamW
from torchvision.datasets import FashionMNIST
import torchvision.transforms as T
from torcheval.metrics.toolkit import sync_and_compute
from fastprogress import progress_bar

from accelerate import Accelerator

```

Store your configuration parameters



```
cfg = SimpleNamespace(
    path=".",
    bs=256,
    epochs=5,
    size=28,
    num_workers=8,
)

WANDB_PROJECT = "accelerate_fmnist"

```

setup transforms



```
tfms = T.Compose([
    T.RandomCrop(cfg.size, padding=1),
    T.RandomHorizontalFlip(),
    T.ToTensor()
])

```

Create a simple CNN



```
def conv_block(in_ch, out_ch, ks=3): return nn.Sequential(nn.BatchNorm2d(in_ch),
                                                    nn.Conv2d(in_ch, out_ch, ks, stride=2, padding=0), 
                                                    nn.ReLU())

def create_cnn():
    return nn.Sequential(nn.Conv2d(1, 16, 5, stride=1, padding="same"),
                         conv_block(16, 32),
                         conv_block(32, 64),
                         conv_block(64, 128),
                         conv_block(128, 256, 1),
                         nn.Sequential(nn.Flatten(), nn.Linear(256,10), nn.BatchNorm1d(10)),
                        )

```

Wrap everything into a training functions (this is necessary to run on multiple GPUS, if it is only one, you can skip the wrapping)



```
def train(cfg):

    # data
    ds = FashionMNIST(cfg.path, transform=tfms, download=True) 
    dl = DataLoader(ds, batch_size=cfg.bs, num_workers=cfg.num_workers)

    # model
    model = create_cnn()

    # training setup
    optimizer = AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)


    # accelerate
    accelerator = Accelerator(log_with="wandb")

    # this will call wandb.init(...)
    accelerator.init_trackers(WANDB_PROJECT, config=cfg)

    # prepare
    model, optimizer, dl = accelerator.prepare(model, optimizer, dl)

    # train
    model.train()
    for epoch in progress_bar(range(cfg.epochs)):
        accurate, num_elems = 0., 0
        for source, targets in dl:
            optimizer.zero_grad()
            output = model(source)
            loss = F.cross_entropy(output, targets)
            accelerator.backward(loss)

            # under the hood this calls wandb.log(...) on the main process
            accelerator.log({"train_loss": loss})

            accurate_preds = output.argmax(dim=1) == targets
            num_elems += accurate_preds.shape[0]
            accurate += accurate_preds.long().sum()
            optimizer.step()
        accuracy = accurate.item() / num_elems
        accelerator.log({"epoch":epoch, "accuracy":accuracy}, log_kwargs={"wandb": {"commit": False}})
        print(f"epoch: {epoch:3} || loss: {loss:5.3f} || accuracy: {accuracy:5.3f}")

    # this will call wandb.finish()
    accelerator.end_training()

```

Let’s train on 2 GPUs! This is really nice, as accelerate will take care of only calling `log` on the main process, so only one run get’s created, so no need to manually check the rank of the process when using multiple GPUs.



```
num_GPUSs = 2

from accelerate import notebook_launcher

notebook_launcher(train, (cfg,), num_processes=num_GPUSs)

```

This can sometimes be confusing, and you may want to log only on the main process. To do so, you will have to detect in which process you are manually and avoid creating runs (calling `wandb.init` in all other processes)

wandb.require(experiment="service")
path = rank0_first(lambda: untar_data(URLs.PETS) / "images")


def train():
    cb = []
    dls = ImageDataLoaders.from_name_func(
        path,
        get_image_files(path),
        valid_pct=0.2,
        label_func=lambda x: x[0].isupper(),
        item_tfms=Resize(224),
    )
    if rank_distrib() == 0:
        run = wandb.init("fastai_ddp", entity="capecape")
        cb = WandbCallback()
    learn = vision_learner(dls, resnet34, metrics=error_rate, cbs=cb).to_fp16()
    with learn.distrib_ctx(sync_bn=False):
        learn.fit(1)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    train()

```

question: i think i might have a bug with logging distributed training, how can I do it with wandb?
answer:
When logging distributed training with `wandb`, you have a couple of options depending on how you want to organize your logs. Here are the general approaches you can take:

### One Process (Recommended)
- **Initialize `wandb` in a single process**: This is typically the main process or the process with rank 0 in distributed training.
- **Log from the main process**: Use `wandb.log` only in the main process to log your metrics.
- **Collect data from other processes**: If you need to log information from other processes, you can send that data to the main process using inter-process communication methods like queues or pipes.

### Many Processes
- **Initialize `wandb` in every process**: Call `wandb.init` in each process of your distributed training setup.
- **Group runs together**: Use the `group` argument in `wandb.init` to group all the runs together in the W&B UI. This allows you to compare and aggregate metrics across all processes.
- **Log independently**: Each process logs its own metrics independently using `wandb.log`.

### Example for PyTorch Distributed Data Parallel (DDP)
Here's a simplified example of how you might set up `wandb` with PyTorch DDP:

```python
import torch.distributed as dist
import wandb

def is_main_process():
    return dist.get_rank() == 0

def setup(rank, world_size):
    # setup distributed training environment
    dist.init_process_group("nccl", rank=rank, world_size=world_size)

def cleanup():
    dist.destroy_process_group()

def train(rank, world_size):
    setup(rank, world_size)
    
    # Initialize wandb only in the main process
    if is_main_process():
        wandb.init(project="my_distributed_project", group="experiment_xyz")
    
    # Your training code here
    # ...

    # Log metrics only from the main process
    if is_main_process():
        wandb.log({"loss": loss_value})
    
    cleanup()

def main():
    world_size = 2  # Number of processes
    torch.multiprocessing.spawn(train, args=(world_size,), nprocs=world_size)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

### Troubleshooting Tips
- Ensure that `wandb.init()` is called before any logging happens.
- Make sure that `wandb.log()` is only called from processes where `wandb.init()` has been called.
- If you're using the "Many Processes" approach, ensure that each process has a unique `name` or `id` and the same `group` name to properly group them in the UI.
- Check for any errors or warnings in the output that might indicate why logging isn't working as expected.
- Review the W&B documentation for any framework-specific integration details or examples.

If you're still encountering issues, you can reach out to W&B support with details about your setup, the code you're using, and the behavior you're observing. They may be able to provide more targeted assistance.

Source:	https://docs.wandb.ai/guides/track/tracking-faq

How can I use wandb with multiprocessing, e.g. distributed training?


If your training program uses multiple processes you will need to structure your program to avoid making wandb method calls from processes where you did not run `wandb.init()`.\  

\  

There are several approaches to managing multiprocess training:


1. Call `wandb.init` in all your processes, using the group keyword argument to define a shared group. Each process will have its own wandb run and the UI will group the training processes together.
2. Call `wandb.init` from just one process and pass data to be logged over multiprocessing queues.


:::info  

Check out the Distributed Training Guide for more detail on these two approaches, including code examples with Torch DDP.  

:::

---

Source:	https://docs.wandb.ai/guides/track/log/distributed-training

Log distributed training experiments


  

Log distributed training experiments  




In distributed training, models are trained using multiple GPUs in parallel. W&B supports two patterns to track distributed training experiments:


1. **One process**: Initialize W&B (`wandb.init`) and log experiments (`wandb.log`) from a single process. This is a common solution for logging distributed training experiments with the PyTorch Distributed Data Parallel (DDP) Class. In some cases, users funnel data over from other processes using a multiprocessing queue (or another communication primitive) to the main logging process.
2. **Many processes**: Initialize W&B (`wandb.init`) and log experiments (`wandb.log`) in every process. Each process is effectively a separate experiment. Use the `group` parameter when you initialize W&B (`wandb.init(group='group-name')`) to define a shared experiment and group the logged values together in the W&B App UI.


The proceeding examples demonstrate how to track metrics with W&B using PyTorch DDP on two GPUs on a single machine. PyTorch DDP (`DistributedDataParallel` in`torch.nn`) is a popular library for distributed training. The basic principles apply to any distributed training setup, but the details of implementation may differ.


:::info  

Explore the code behind these examples in the W&B GitHub examples repository here. Specifically, see the `log-dpp.py` Python script for information on how to implement one process and many process methods.  

:::

question: Explain in detail how can I use wandb to train a LLM and is there any way to log prompts keeping them compatible with tools like LangChainAI, OpenAI, Huggingface Transformers, etc.
answer:
Weights & Biases (wandb) is a machine learning platform that helps you track experiments, visualize data, and share insights. It is not a training platform itself but rather a tool that integrates with your existing machine learning workflow to provide additional features such as experiment tracking, dataset versioning, and model evaluation. You can use wandb to train a Large Language Model (LLM) by integrating it with your training scripts.

Here's how you can use wandb to train an LLM:

1. **Set Up wandb**: Before you start training, you need to install the wandb library and log in to your account. You can install wandb using pip:

   ```bash
   pip install wandb
   ```

   Then, log in to your wandb account:

   ```bash
   wandb login
   ```

2. **Configure wandb in Your Training Script**: In your training script, you need to import wandb and initialize it. This typically involves calling `wandb.init()` and optionally passing in parameters like `project`, `name`, and `entity` to organize your runs.

   ```python
   import wandb

   wandb.init(project="my-llm-project", name="llm-training-run", entity="my-entity")
   ```

3. **Log Hyperparameters and Metrics**: As you set up your LLM for training, you can log hyperparameters using `wandb.config` and metrics using `wandb.log`.

   ```python
   wandb.config.learning_rate = 0.001
   wandb.config.batch_size = 32

   # During training, log metrics
   for epoch in range(num_epochs):
       loss = train()  # Your training loop here
       wandb.log({"epoch": epoch, "loss": loss})
   ```

4. **Log Model Checkpoints**: If you want to log model checkpoints, you can use the `wandb.save` function to save model weights to the wandb server.

   ```python
   model.save("model.h5")
   wandb.save("model.h5")
   ```

5. **Visualize and Analyze**: Once your training is complete, you can use the wandb web interface to visualize and analyze the training process. You can compare different runs, inspect model performance, and share your results with others.

Regarding logging prompts and keeping them compatible with tools like LangChainAI, OpenAI, and Huggingface Transformers, wandb allows you to log arbitrary data, including text. You can log prompts as text data and then analyze them in the wandb interface. Here's an example of how you might log prompts:

```python
# Assume 'prompts' is a list of strings that you're using with your LLM
for prompt in prompts:
    wandb.log({"prompt": prompt})
```

To ensure compatibility with other tools, you would need to structure your logging in a way that matches the expected input format for those tools. For instance, if you're using a specific JSON format for prompts with LangChainAI, you would log the JSON object directly. The key is to log the data in a structured format that can be easily parsed and used by the tool you're integrating with.

Remember that wandb is a flexible tool, and you can log almost any type of data. However, it's essential to be mindful of privacy and security when logging data, especially if your prompts contain sensitive information. Wandb provides features to control access to your data and keep it secure.

W&B Prompts is a suite of LLMOps tools built for the development of LLM-powered applications. Training 🏋️


Using wandb just requires configuring the command line argument `--logger wandb`. This automatically turns on the wandb logger for your experiment and further arguments can be added -


1. `wandb-project`: To specify the project in which experiment is being run.
2. `wandb-run`: The name of the wandb run
3. `wandb-entity`: Entity which is starting the run
4. `wandb-log_checkpoints`: True/False to log model checkpoints to the wandb dashboard
5. `wandb-num_eval_images`: Number of images from the validation set to be logged to wandb. Predictions corresponding to these can be visualized on the dashboard. No images are logged if the value is 0 and all are logged if the value is -1.

question: how does Weights & Biases know that the actual dataset in S3 has not changed even though the version of the object has been updated in S3, thereby preventing Weights & Biases from creating a new version of the artifact ?
answer:
Weights & Biases (W&B) uses checksumming to determine whether the actual data in an artifact has changed, regardless of where the data is stored, such as in an S3 bucket. When you log an artifact in W&B, it computes a checksum of the artifact's contents. If you log the same artifact again, W&B will compute a new checksum for the current contents.

If the checksum of the artifact's contents has not changed, W&B knows that the data has not been altered, and it will not create a new version of the artifact. This is true even if the version of the object in S3 has been updated. The version update in S3 might be due to metadata changes or other non-content-related updates that do not affect the actual data.

When you use artifact references, W&B records metadata about the S3/GCS object, such as its ETag, size, and version ID. The ETag is particularly useful because it often acts as a hash of the object's contents. If the ETag has not changed, W&B can infer that the contents have likely not changed either. However, it's important to note that the ETag is not a strict checksum and may not change even if the contents have changed in certain cases, such as with multi-part uploads.

In summary, W&B relies on checksums and metadata like ETags to determine if the data within an artifact has changed. If the underlying data is the same, W&B will not create a new artifact version, even if the S3 object's version has been updated.

Source:	https://docs.wandb.ai/guides/artifacts/create-a-new-artifact-version

Create new artifact versions from scratch


There are two ways to create a new artifact version: from a single run and from distributed runs. They are defined as follows:


* **Single run**: A single run provides all the data for a new version. This is the most common case and is best suited when the run fully recreates the needed data. For example: outputting saved models or model predictions in a table for analysis.
* **Distributed runs**: A set of runs collectively provides all the data for a new version. This is best suited for distributed jobs which have multiple runs generating data, often in parallel. For example: evaluating a model in a distributed manner, and outputting the predictions.


W&B will create a new artifact and assign it a `v0` alias if you pass a name to the `wandb.Artifact` API that does not exist in your project. W&B checksums the contents when you log again to the same artifact. If the artifact changed, W&B saves a new version `v1`. 


W&B will retrieve an existing artifact if you pass a name and artifact type to the `wandb.Artifact` API that matches an existing artifact in your project. The retrieved artifact will have a version greater than 1.

---

question: How can you add weights & biases to classification/object detection using Tensorflow Model Maker?
answer:
TensorFlow Model Maker is a library that simplifies the process of adapting and converting TensorFlow models to run on mobile and edge devices. It provides a high-level API for TensorFlow Lite that supports tasks like image classification, object detection, text classification, and question answering.

To integrate Weights & Biases (W&B) with TensorFlow Model Maker for tasks like classification or object detection, you would typically follow these steps:

1. **Install Weights & Biases**: If you haven't already, install the W&B package using pip:

```python
!pip install wandb
```

2. **Initialize W&B**: Before starting your training process, initialize W&B by calling `wandb.init()`. This will start a new W&B run and create a dashboard for your project.

```python
import wandb
wandb.init(project='my-tf-model-maker-project', entity='my-wandb-username')
```

3. **Configure W&B**: Set up your configuration for the run. This can include hyperparameters, model architecture details, dataset information, etc.

```python
config = wandb.config
config.learning_rate = 0.001
config.batch_size = 32
config.epochs = 10
# Add other hyperparameters or settings as needed
```

4. **Log Metrics**: During training, you can log metrics to W&B. TensorFlow Model Maker may not have a direct callback for W&B, so you might need to manually log the metrics after each epoch or training step.

```python
model = model_maker_object.create(train_data, model_spec=spec, epochs=config.epochs, batch_size=config.batch_size)

# Assuming 'model' is a Keras model and 'history' is the output of the fit function
history = model.fit(train_data, validation_data=val_data, epochs=config.epochs)

# Log metrics to W&B after each epoch
for epoch in range(config.epochs):
    wandb.log({
        'epoch': epoch,
        'loss': history.history['loss'][epoch],
        'accuracy': history.history['accuracy'][epoch],
        'val_loss': history.history['val_loss'][epoch],
        'val_accuracy': history.history['val_accuracy'][epoch]
    })
```

5. **Save and Log Artifacts**: You can save your model and log it as an artifact in W&B.

```python
# Save the TensorFlow Lite model
model.export(export_dir='.', with_metadata=True)

# Log the model as an artifact
artifact = wandb.Artifact('model', type='model')
artifact.add_file('model.tflite')
wandb.log_artifact(artifact)
```

6. **Finish the Run**: Once training is complete, call `wandb.finish()` to close the W&B run and finalize the logging.

```python
wandb.finish()
```

Please note that the above code is a general guideline for integrating W&B with TensorFlow Model Maker. The actual implementation may vary based on the specific version of TensorFlow Model Maker you are using and the task at hand (classification or object detection). If TensorFlow Model Maker provides a callback mechanism similar to Keras, you could create a custom callback that logs the desired metrics to W&B.

question: where can I find a schema for the yaml or json definition of a sweep plan
answer:
The schema for defining a sweep configuration in YAML or JSON is not typically provided as a formal schema file like an XSD or JSON Schema. Instead, the structure and options for the configuration are documented in the Weights & Biases (W&B) documentation and examples.

For YAML, the configuration is defined in a YAML file with specific keys and values that correspond to the sweep's settings. For JSON, the configuration would be a nested dictionary in Python that can be easily converted to a JSON object.

Here are the key components you would typically include in a sweep configuration:

1. **method**: The search strategy to use (e.g., `grid`, `random`, `bayes`).
2. **metric**: The metric to optimize, including its `name` and `goal` (e.g., `minimize` or `maximize`).
3. **parameters**: A dictionary of hyperparameters to tune, where each key is a hyperparameter name, and the value describes how to sample it (e.g., a list of `values`, a `min` and `max` for a range, or a distribution to sample from).
4. **early_terminate**: (Optional) Criteria for early termination of poorly performing runs.
5. **name**: (Optional) A name for the sweep.

Here's an example of what a YAML configuration might look like:

```yaml
method: bayes
metric:
  name: validation_loss
  goal: minimize
parameters:
  learning_rate:
    min: 0.0001
    max: 0.1
  optimizer:
    values: ["adam", "sgd"]
```

And here's an equivalent JSON configuration in a Python dictionary:

```python
sweep_config = {
    "method": "bayes",
    "metric": {
        "name": "validation_loss",
        "goal": "minimize"
    },
    "parameters": {
        "learning_rate": {
            "min": 0.0001,
            "max": 0.1
        },
        "optimizer": {
            "values": ["adam", "sgd"]
        }
    }
}
```

To convert this Python dictionary to JSON, you could use the `json` module:

```python
import json

json_config = json.dumps(sweep_config, indent=2)
print(json_config)
```

For the most up-to-date and detailed information on the configuration options available for W&B sweeps, you should refer to the official W&B documentation, which you can find at https://docs.wandb.ai/guides/sweeps/configuration. This documentation will provide you with the necessary information to construct a valid sweep configuration.
context:
Source:	https://docs.wandb.ai/guides/sweeps/define-sweep-configuration

Define sweep configuration


  

Define sweep configuration for hyperparameter tuning.  




A W&B Sweep combines a strategy for exploring hyperparameter values with the code that evaluates them. The strategy can be as simple as trying every option or as complex as Bayesian Optimization and Hyperband (BOHB).


Define your strategy in the form of a sweep configuration. Specify the configuration either in a:


1. Python nested dictionary data structure if you use a Jupyter Notebook or Python script.
2. YAML file if you use the command line (CLI).


The following code snippets demonstrate examples of how to define a Sweep configuration in a Jupyter Notebook or Python script or within a YAML file. Configuration keys are defined in detail in subsequent sections.


  

  

 Within your Jupyter Notebook or Python script, define a sweep in a dictionary Python data structure.



```
sweep_configuration = {
    "method": "bayes",
    "name": "sweep",
    "metric": {"goal": "minimize", "name": "validation_loss"},
    "parameters": {
        "batch_size": {"values": [16, 32, 64]},
        "epochs": {"values": [5, 10, 15]},
        "lr": {"max": 0.1, "min": 0.0001},
    },
}

```

  

  

 Create a map in your YAML where the keys can have, as their values, further keys.



```
program: train.py
method: bayes
metric:
  name: validation_loss
  goal: minimize
parameters:
  learning_rate:
    min: 0.0001
    max: 0.1
  optimizer:
    values: ["adam", "sgd"]

```


:::caution  

1. Ensure that you log (`wandb.log`) the *exact* metric name that you defined the sweep to optimize within your Python script or Jupyter Notebook.  

2. You cannot change the Sweep configuration once you start the W&B Sweep agent.  

:::


For example, suppose you want W&B Sweeps to maximize the validation accuracy during training. Within your Python script you store the validation accuracy in a variable `val_loss`. In your YAML configuration file you define this as:



```
metric:
  goal: maximize
  name: val_loss

```

You must log the variable `val_loss` (in this example) within your Python script or Jupyter Notebook to W&B.



```
wandb.log({"val_loss": validation_loss})

```

Defining the metric in the sweep configuration is only required when using the bayes method for the sweep.

---

Source:	https://github.com/wandb/examples/tree/master/colabs/keras/Keras_param_opti_using_sweeps.ipynb

4️⃣ Define the Sweep


Fundamentally, a Sweep combines a strategy for trying out a bunch of hyperparameter values with the code that evalutes them.  

Whether that strategy is as simple as trying every option  

or as complex as BOHB,  

Weights & Biases Sweeps have you covered.  

You just need to *define your strategy*  

in the form of a configuration.


When you’re setting up a Sweep in a notebook like this,  

that config object is a nested dictionary.  

When you run a Sweep via the command line,  

the config object is a  

YAML file.


Let’s walk through the definition of a Sweep config together.

this step would be done in a single assignment.

---

Source:	https://github.com/wandb/examples/tree/master/colabs/boosting/Using_W&B_Sweeps_with_XGBoost.ipynb

1. Define the Sweep


Weights & Biases sweeps give you powerful levers to configure your sweeps exactly how you want them, with just a few lines of code. The sweeps config can be defined as  

a dictionary or a YAML file.


Let’s walk through some of them together:  

\* **Metric** – This is the metric the sweeps are attempting to optimize. Metrics can take a `name` (this metric should be logged by your training script) and a `goal` (`maximize` or `minimize`).   

\* **Search Strategy** – Specified using the `"method"` key. We support several different search strategies with sweeps.   

 \* **Grid Search** – Iterates over every combination of hyperparameter values.  

 \* **Random Search** – Iterates over randomly chosen combinations of hyperparameter values.  

 \* **Bayesian Search** – Creates a probabilistic model that maps hyperparameters to probability of a metric score, and chooses parameters with high probability of improving the metric. The objective of Bayesian optimization is to spend more time in picking the hyperparameter values, but in doing so trying out fewer hyperparameter values.  

\* **Parameters** – A dictionary containing the hyperparameter names, and discrete values, a range, or distributions from which to pull their values on each iteration.


You can find a list of all configuration options here.



```
sweep_config = {
    "method": "random", # try grid or random
    "metric": {
      "name": "accuracy",
      "goal": "maximize"   
    },
    "parameters": {
        "booster": {
            "values": ["gbtree","gblinear"]
        },
        "max_depth": {
            "values": [3, 6, 9, 12]
        },
        "learning_rate": {
            "values": [0.1, 0.05, 0.2]
        },
        "subsample": {
            "values": [1, 0.5, 0.3]
        }
    }
}

```

question: please explain the use of datum when constructing custom charts. I would like an explanation of when to explicitly use `datum` in custom charts. The custom line chart provided by wandb uses datum.
answer:
In the context of constructing custom charts, particularly when using a visualization grammar like Vega (which is often used for creating custom visualizations in platforms like W&B), the term `datum` refers to a single data point or an object containing data fields. When you are defining a custom chart, you often need to specify how each visual element of the chart relates to the underlying data. This is where `datum` comes into play.

When you explicitly use `datum` in custom charts, you are typically accessing or manipulating the value of a specific field in a single data point. This is common in scenarios where you need to define expressions or conditions that depend on the data values.

Here are some situations where you might explicitly use `datum` in custom charts:

1. **Conditional Logic**: When you want to apply conditional logic to determine the properties of a visual element, such as its color, size, or visibility, based on the value of the data. For example, you might want to color a point red if its value is above a certain threshold.

2. **Tooltip Content**: When customizing tooltips, you might use `datum` to display the value of a data point when the user hovers over a visual element in the chart.

3. **Calculated Fields**: If you need to create a new field that is a function of existing data fields, you can use `datum` to reference the fields in your calculation.

4. **Axis Labels and Titles**: When setting up axes or titles that should display values from the data, `datum` can be used to dynamically set these text elements based on the data.

5. **Data-Driven Styles**: If the style of a visual element should change based on the data, such as a line's thickness or a marker's shape, you can use `datum` to bind these style properties to data values.

Here's an example of how `datum` might be used in a Vega specification for a custom chart:

```json
{
  "mark": "point",
  "encoding": {
    "x": {"field": "x_field", "type": "quantitative"},
    "y": {"field": "y_field", "type": "quantitative"},
    "color": {
      "condition": {
        "test": "datum.y_field > 10",
        "value": "red"
      },
      "value": "blue"
    }
  }
}
```

In this example, the color of each point is determined by a condition that uses `datum` to reference the value of the `y_field` field in the data. If the value is greater than 10, the point is colored red; otherwise, it is colored blue.

In summary, `datum` is used in custom chart specifications to reference and manipulate the values of individual data points. It is a key part of defining the dynamic behavior of visual elements based on the underlying data.

First, log data in your script. Use wandb.config for single points set at the beginning of training, like hyperparameters. Use wandb.log() for multiple points over time, and log custom 2D arrays with wandb.Table(). We recommend logging up to 10,000 data points per logged key.

Try a quick example notebook to log the data tables, and in the next step we’ll set up custom charts. See what the resulting charts look like in the live report.

---

Source:	https://docs.wandb.ai/guides/app/features/panels/line-plot/

Settings


**Data**


* **X axis**: Select default x-axes including Step and Relative Time, or select a custom x-axis. If you’d like to use a custom x-axis, make sure it’s logged in the same call to `wandb.log()` that you use to log the y-axis.
* **Relative Time (Wall)** is clock time since the process started, so if you started a run and resumed it a day later and logged something that would be plotted a 24hrs.
* **Relative Time (Process)** is time inside the running process, so if you started a run and ran for 10 seconds and resumed a day later that point would be plotted at 10s
* **Wall Time** is minutes elapsed since the start of the first run on the graph
* **Step** increments by default each time `wandb.log()` is called, and is supposed to reflect the number of training steps you’ve logged from your model
* **Y axes**: Select y-axes from the logged values, including metrics and hyperparameters that change over time.
* **Min, max, and log scale**: Minimum, maximum, and log scale settings for x axis and y axis in line plots
* **Smoothing and exclude outliers**: Change the smoothing on the line plot or rescale to exclude outliers from the default plot min and max scale
* **Max runs to show**: Show more lines on the line plot at once by increasing this number, which defaults to 10 runs. You’ll see the message “Showing first 10 runs” on the top of the chart if there are more than 10 runs available but the chart is constraining the number visible.
* **Chart type**: Change between a line plot, an area plot, and a percentage area plot


**X Axis Settings**  

The x-axis can be set at the graph level, as well as globally for the project page or report page. Here’s what the global settings look like:



:::info  

Pick **multiple y-axes** in the line plot settings to compare different metrics on the same chart, like accuracy and validation accuracy for example.  

:::


**Grouping**


* Turn on grouping to see settings for visualizing averaged values.
* **Group key**: Select a column, and all the runs with the same value in that column will be grouped together.
* **Agg**: Aggregation— the value of the line on the graph. The options are mean, median, min, and max of the group.
* **Range**: Switch the behavior for the shaded area behind the grouped curve. None means there is no shaded area. Min/Max shows a shaded region that covers the whole range of points in the group. Std Dev shows the standard deviation of values in the group. Std Err shows the standard error as the shaded area.
* **Sampled runs**: If you have hundreds of runs selected, we default to only sampling the first 100. You can select to have all your runs included in the grouping calculation, but it might slow things down in the UI.


**Legend**


* **Title**: Add a custom title for line plot, which shows up at the top of the chart
* **X-Axis title**: Add a custom title for the x-axis of the line plot, which shows up in the lower right corner of the chart.
* **Y-Axis title**: Add a custom title for the y-axis of the line plot, which shows up in the upper left corner of the chart.
* **Legend**: Select field that you want to see in the legend of the plot for each line. You could, for example, show the name of the run and the learning rate.
* **Legend template**: Fully customizable, this powerful template allows you to specify exactly what text and variables you want to show up in the template at the top of the line plot as well as the legend that appears when you hover your mouse over the plot.



**Expressions**


* **Y Axis Expressions**: Add calculated metrics to your graph. You can use any of the logged metrics as well as configuration values like hyperparameters to calculate custom lines.
* **X Axis Expressions**: Rescale the x-axis to use calculated values using custom expressions. Useful variables include\*\*\_step\*\* for the default x-axis, and the syntax for referencing summary values is `${summary:value}`

question:
I try to create a table with the columns ["full_path", "audio", "speaker_ID", "sentence", "label" ]. Everything works fine, but the problem is that I have around 8k audiofiles. 
I work using google colab, and during the process of the table creation (around half of the process duration) the system crashes - not enough RAM (12 Gb). Seems like it firstly loads all audio into the RAM and only then uploads it to the W and B.  if it is already more than 12 Gb in RAM, the final table on the W and B will be the same size then?
answer:
When you create a `wandb.Table` and add data to it, the data is indeed loaded into RAM before being uploaded to Weights & Biases (W&B). If you have a large number of audio files, or if the files are large, this can quickly consume a lot of memory, leading to the crashes you're experiencing on Google Colab.

The final table size on W&B will depend on the actual data you're logging. If you're logging references to the audio files (i.e., file paths) rather than the audio data itself, the table will be smaller. However, if you're uploading the actual audio data (e.g., by converting the audio files to `wandb.Audio` objects), the table size will be roughly the same as the total size of the audio data.

To avoid running out of RAM, you can try the following strategies:

1. **Log in Batches**: Instead of adding all audio files to the table at once, you can add them in smaller batches. After each batch, you can log the table as an artifact and then clear the table to free up memory.

2. **Use File Paths**: If possible, log the file paths to the audio files instead of the audio data itself. This will significantly reduce the memory footprint. You can upload the audio files separately as artifacts and then reference them in your table.

3. **Increase Virtual Memory**: Google Colab provides a limited amount of RAM, but you can sometimes work around this by using disk space as virtual memory. This will slow down your processing but can prevent crashes due to RAM limitations.

4. **Stream Data**: If W&B supports streaming data, you could stream the audio data directly to W&B without loading everything into memory first. This would require using W&B's APIs in a way that supports streaming, which may not be available for all data types.

5. **Use a Local Runtime**: If you have a local machine with more memory, you can connect your Colab notebook to a local runtime to leverage your machine's resources.

6. **Optimize Data**: Before logging, ensure that the audio files are in a format and bitrate that balance quality with file size to reduce the overall data footprint.

Here's an example of how you might log in batches (this is a conceptual example and may need to be adapted to your specific use case):

```python
import wandb

# Initialize a W&B run
run = wandb.init(project="your_project", job_type="data_logging")

# Create a table with the desired columns
table = wandb.Table(columns=["full_path", "audio", "speaker_ID", "sentence", "label"])

# Define batch size
batch_size = 100  # Adjust this number based on your available memory

# Process and log audio files in batches
for i, audio_file in enumerate(audio_files):
    # Process your audio file here
    # ...
    
    # Add data to the table
    table.add_data(full_path, wandb.Audio(audio_file), speaker_ID, sentence, label)
    
    # Log the table in batches
    if (i + 1) % batch_size == 0 or (i + 1) == len(audio_files):
        # Log the current batch as an artifact
        artifact = wandb.Artifact("audio_data", type="dataset")
        artifact.add(table, "audio_batch_{}".format(i // batch_size))
        run.log_artifact(artifact)
        
        # Clear the table to free up memory
        table = wandb.Table(columns=["full_path", "audio", "speaker_ID", "sentence", "label"])

# Finish the W&B run
run.finish()
```

Remember to adjust the `batch_size` to a value that your environment can handle without running out of memory.

def create_example(image_file, label):
    feature = {
        "image": tf.train.Feature(
            bytes_list=tf.train.BytesList(
                value=[tf.io.read_file(image_file).numpy()]
            )
        ),
        "label": tf.train.Feature(
            int64_list=tf.train.Int64List(value=[LABEL_DICT[label][1]])
        ),
        "label_name": tf.train.Feature(
            bytes_list=tf.train.BytesList(
                value=[LABEL_DICT[label][0].encode('utf8')]
            )
        )
    }
    return tf.train.Example(
        features=tf.train.Features(feature=feature)
    )

    label_chunks = chunkify(labels, max_chunk_size)
    table = wandb.Table(columns=[
        "Image", "Label-Name", "Label-ID", "Split-Name", "Chunk-ID"
    ])
    for idx in range(num_chunks):
        image_chunk = image_chunks[idx]
        label_chunk = label_chunks[idx]
        current_chunk_size = len(image_chunk)
        file_name = "%.2i-%.3i.tfrec" % (idx + 1, current_chunk_size)
        tfrecord_file = os.path.join(dump_dir, file_name)
        writer = tf.io.TFRecordWriter(tfrecord_file)
        progress_bar = tqdm(
            range(current_chunk_size),
            desc=f"Writing {file_name}"
        )
        for chunk_idx in progress_bar:
            image = Image.open(image_chunk[chunk_idx])
            table.add_data(
                wandb.Image(image),
                LABEL_DICT[label_chunk[chunk_idx]][0],
                LABEL_DICT[label_chunk[chunk_idx]][1],
                dump_dir.split("/")[-1],
                idx
            )
            example = create_example(
                image_chunk[chunk_idx], label_chunk[chunk_idx]
            )
            writer.write(example.SerializeToString())
        writer.close()
    return table

```

def parse_tfrecord(example):

        example, {
            "image": tf.io.FixedLenFeature([], tf.string),

            "label_name": tf.io.VarLenFeature(tf.string)

    example["label"] = tf.sparse.to_dense(example["label"])

question: How can I log my results in my team profile and not in my personal profile?
answer:
To log your results to your team profile instead of your personal profile, you should use the `entity` parameter to specify your team's name when setting up your W&B logger. Here's how you can do it:

1. Make sure you are a member of the team on W&B.
2. Use the `entity` parameter to specify your team's name when initializing your W&B run.

For example, if you're using the `wandb.init()` function in your code, you would do something like this:

```python
import wandb

wandb.init(project='your_project_name', entity='YOUR_TEAM_NAME')
```

Replace `'your_project_name'` with the name of the project you're working on and `'YOUR_TEAM_NAME'` with the actual name of your team on W&B.

By specifying the `entity` parameter, all the data logged with this run will be associated with your team's profile rather than your personal profile. This is important when you are collaborating with others and want to ensure that the results are shared with the team and contribute to the team's collective work.
